title: Merge libpod funcionality into CRI-O
body: It appears that buildah is limited to working on containers it creates itself.  One direction we are considering for the OpenShift build side is to execute builds only within containers created by the existing runtime (crio or docker).  Can buildah perform commands against other containers, and if not, what would it take to support that?

comments.body:
Not sure how this would work.  Theoretically buildah should be able to work with CRI-O Containers since they are using the same storage back end.  Buildah would have to learn how to do this, but this seems like a nice feature.    Do you just want to do a `buildah run` command and `buildah commit` or all other commands.  

Getting this to work with Docker containers would be more difficult, it could use runc to exec into the container, but that is about it.

The two things I could imagine would be:

1. committing a running cri-o / runc container image (something using container/storage)
2. running could be done via `runc enter`

Being able to commit an existing container into an image would probably sufficient by itself (all the others can be implemented other ways).
Once we have the three tools integrated into libpod, we should be able to podman commit or buildah commit the container into an image.

buildah can "commit" a cri-o container's root filesystem today.  Sure, you end up with an image whose runtime configuration is what was inherited from the image that was used to create the container, but sometimes that's enough.

Specifying which container you mean to do that with is a bit complicated because the name stored with the container at the level that `buildah` sees it is not the value we get back from `crictl ps`, but rather that of the container's `io.kubernetes.cri-o.Name` annotation.  That value is more useful for avoiding name collisions.  It can either be dug up using `runc list` or other tools, or the container's ID can be specified instead.

Those containers don't normally show up in the list produced by `buildah containers` because it usually suppresses entries for containers for which buildah can't find the state data (currently a file named buildah.json) that it creates when it creates a container root filesystem.  However, those containers are listed by `buildah containers -a`, and they can be distinguished from others by the lack of an asterisk in the BUILDER column of the output.

Using something like `buildah config` to start tweaking the configuration that we will be written when committing will force buildah to initialize its state data for the container (i.e., create the buildah.json file), and after that point the container will show up in the output of `buildah containers` even without the `-a` flag.

I've hit this issue trying to use `podman build`. I decided to use an old Dockerfile I had that had FROM fedora:latest. It was smart enough to fid that I had already downloaded the image to container-storage but that image doesn't have a buildah.json file associated. 

Furthermore I notice that podman looks for these buildah.json files in containers/storage/overlay-containers/<id> and not containers/storage/overlay-images/<id>   That seems a bit backward, no?

I think maybe the above is the problem. If you remove the container associated with the image then the buildah.json file is gone even though the image is still there. So it finds the image and tries to find the container that is associated it with (?) i.e. it assumes all images have a container.

I'm going to do a bit more testing on this - maybe if I commit the container that I got from `buildah from` It will generate a build.json file. (?) .  Where does the buildah.json file get build? Where is the call? It seems that it might be just a matter of calling it after the pull is complete. (?)

I think this is probably more of an issue for podman. Once podman starts sharing the database with cri-o, then podman commit could do what you want.
@mheon WDYT
@rhatdan If our usecase is committing existing containers running by CRI-O, `podman commit` once we have them sharing containers should be perfect.
I don't know if there is a way to shift an issue from one REPO to antother.  @mheon Can you open this issue in podman and link @smarterclayton to it.
@rhatdan A specific issue for committing CRI-O containers? Or should we use the generic CRI-O integration tracker bug in podman for this?

I would say a specific issue on committing CRI-O containers, then we can close this one with a link to it.  Theoretically we could get to the point of committing CRI-O containers before we get to full integration.
`podman commit` can do this now, well will be able to do it once CRI-O and podman share the same library.
Sadly this is still in the same state.  Although some sub-packages of libpod are starting to get added to CRI-O
We have opened talks on this again.
I am closing this issue in favor of https://github.com/containers/libpod/issues/2791
